Speaker 0: even though the light goes out and you see the light go out,

Speaker 0: the problem could be somewhere in the wiring down the line, correct? Yes. Now looking at the human body,

Speaker 0: um, if you have a compressed nerve in the lower back

Speaker 0: because that nerve root exits the foramenot in the lumbar spine and goes down the legs,

Speaker 0: at the, uh, in the lower back,

Speaker 0: spine

Speaker 0: at the neuro, at the neural foramen in the neck, the patient could experience,

Speaker 0: uh, radicular symptoms in the shoulders and the arms even though the impingement is actually in the neck because of where the nerves go after they leave the neck, correct? Yes.

Speaker 0: Now I read your report in this case,

Speaker 0: mentioned

Speaker 0: that

Speaker 0: the plaintiff in this case, Ms. Vargas showed signs of disc degeneration Bentley: in her cervical and lumbar spine, correct? Yes. Okay. What is disc degeneration? Can you please explain that to the jury?

Speaker 1: Disc degeneration

Speaker 1: is

Speaker 1: a natural condition that happens in

Speaker 1: patients

Speaker 1: as they age, although I've got 15 year olds who have it too. But

Speaker 1: typically, it's associated with

Speaker 1: aging.

Speaker 1: It's a wear and tear type phenomenon where

Speaker 1: your disc that's made of 2 different structures, 1 is the soft

Speaker 1: cushioning material in the center and then this fibrous material on the outside.

Speaker 1: Over time,

Speaker 1: the cells inside the soft cushioning material stop secreting proteins.

Speaker 1: Those proteins essentially

Speaker 1: act like a wick and suck water into

Speaker 1: the disc. And when you're stopping the protein, the disc dries out. It loses some of its ability to cushion.

Speaker 1: You also start getting that same

Speaker 1: change in mechanics starts affecting the fibrous material around it. And over time,

Speaker 1: in the degenerated disc, the fibrous material

Speaker 1: thin out,

Speaker 1: and then they start changing their shape.

Speaker 1: And

Speaker 1: they start kind of pooching out some.

Speaker 1: They also start losing height.

Speaker 1: Over time, it can be the point where the disc completely disappears.

Speaker 1: That sort of goes from just a little bit of a pooch maybe a disc herniation to just completely going away and wearing out. So that's disc degeneration that's commonly seen in people.

Speaker 0: And disc degeneration

Speaker 0: in layman's terms,

Speaker 1: that can be described as the normal wear and tear of the spine as you get older and use the spine, correct? It can be normal or it can, I mean we treat, that's what we treat as spine surgeons, mainly is disc degeneration? So it be

Speaker 1: symptomatic at some point, but yes, but people get it over time. And

Speaker 0: part of that's just as the body grows old and wears out,

Speaker 0: the spine is a joint, correct?

Speaker 0: Yes. And

Speaker 0: just as you move and use your body throughout your lifetime,

Speaker 0: as the spine wears out, 1 of the things that you experience would be disc degeneration of the spine, correct? Yes. And

Speaker 0: Ms. Vargas was approximately 57 years old at the time of this crash, correct? Yes.

Speaker 0: For the disc degeneration that you did know in the MRIs of her spine, was that normal for a 57 year old woman?

Speaker 1: Pretty much, yes.

Speaker 1: 57

Speaker 1: year olds, about 50 percent to 60 percent of them have this degeneration. So yes. Okay. And people,

Speaker 0: especially

Speaker 0: people of Ms. Vargas' age,

Speaker 0: many people out there have disc degeneration that is not creating any pain whatsoever, correct? Yes.

Speaker 0: That's called asymptomatic,

Speaker 0: know no symptoms means asymptomatic,

Speaker 0: correct? Yes.

Speaker 0: And

Speaker 0: is it not true that trauma

Speaker 0: can cause distant degeneration

Speaker 0: to become symptomatic when it was previously asymptomatic?

Speaker 1: I think absent any objective

Speaker 1: evidence of acute findings, no. That's

Speaker 1: kind of a thing where I don't think just because you've been in trauma, discs that are previously degenerated and asymptomatic

Speaker 1: become symptomatic

Speaker 1: with no objective evidence of change in them.

Speaker 1: So you can't look the same and it starts hurting just because you're in a car accident.

Speaker 0: But there is peer reviewed literature out there that says that following a car wreck,

Speaker 0: disc degeneration can become symptomatic

Speaker 0: without acute findings on the MRIs,

Speaker 1: correct? Dr. I am not aware of that literature.

Speaker 0: Are you familiar with an organization called the National Library for Medicine?

Speaker 1: National Library for Medicine. No. Okay.

Speaker 1: PubMed? Yes, I am. Okay.

Speaker 0: Is this a peer reviewed document? Well, let me

Speaker 1: this article here, have you ever seen this article before?

Speaker 1: An early MRI finding correlates with longstanding symptoms following whiplash perspective wonder, no. Okay.

Speaker 0: I'm going to switch to a PDF copy of it.

Speaker 0: Since And that PDF author website,

Speaker 0: do you see here where it says

Speaker 0: EUR Spine J. 02/2017?

Speaker 0: Yes. It has several names listed down here below it.

Speaker 0: The author's names, yes. Yeah. So I put some highlights here and just

Speaker 0: looking at this,

Speaker 0: it says

Speaker 0: neck pain is the cardinal symptom following whiplash injuries.

Speaker 0: The trauma mechanism could theoretically lead to both soft tissue and bone injury that could be visibly visualized by means of MRI.

Speaker 0: From previous quite

Speaker 0: small trials, it seems the MRI does not demonstrate significant tissue damage. And I'll let you read this whole thing to yourself, doctor, if you would like,

Speaker 0: but it talks about how participants were included after rear end and frontal car collisions

Speaker 0: and it goes on to say that outcome parameters were neck pain, headaches, neck disability,

Speaker 0: and working ability.

Speaker 0: A total of 178

Speaker 0: participants had a cervical MRI scan on average 13 days after the injury.

Speaker 0: Traumatic findings were observed in 7 participants

Speaker 0: sign of disc degeneration were common and most frequent C5, C6 and C6 levels.

Speaker 0: Findings were not associated with outcome after 3 or 12 months. The population had no considerable neck trouble prior to the whiplash injury

Speaker 0: and the non traumatic findings represents findings to be expected in the background population.

Speaker 0: Trauma related MRI findings are rare in whiplash populations

Speaker 0: screened for serious injuries in the emergency unit and not related to specific symptomology.

Speaker 1: Also, generation I'm sorry, because you rolled up a little bit. You're

Speaker 0: Yes. I can't see. I mean, I was I'm sorry. So it goes on to say the population had no considerable neck trouble prior to the whiplash injury and the non traumatic findings represents findings to be expected in the background population.

Speaker 0: Trauma related MRI findings are rare in a whiplash population

Speaker 0: screened for serious injuries in the emergency unit and not related to symptomatic

Speaker 1: Is that a crime? No, you can do that. But in my opinion, people such as Ms. Vargas who have been involved in this car accidents typically do not need injections. So definitely not related to the accident.

Speaker 1: and based on the examination and as well as radiological findings, there's no indication the patient really has pain. But yes, if there's a pattern of behavior where you're prescribing medication that for people who don't need it and you've got running a pill mill without appropriate testing, yes, that's you can potentially get in trouble, potentially. Okay,

Speaker 1: the phrase patient advocate,

Speaker 1: the doctor has a response, the treating physician has a responsibility to be more than a patient advocate. Speaker 1: not only raise that with the patient, but not give them a treatment plan that they are requesting if it is not really going to help them, correct? Dr. Correct.
